exceptions.reason: The plan-acceptance Build entry is Claude Code's plan-mode completion signal; Codex has no equivalent stable signal, and inferring build intent would break the explicit-entry law. On Codex, building starts only by the typed $engine-start — the absence of this hook is the enforcement of that, not a gap.

Claude Code pulls the codes of conduct into every session mechanically (the floor's @import); Codex has no import mechanism, so the AGENTS.md floor carries an imperative required-reading instruction instead — discipline where Claude has mechanics. The session-start briefing (when hooks run) independently carries the orientation, so the instruction is the fallback path, not the only one.

On Claude, the audit and plan-review personas mechanically denylist shell commands; the Codex agent format has no per-tool denylist field, so the renders carry the prohibition as instruction text inside a read-only sandbox (the sandbox bounds the blast radius; the wording is discipline).

Codex skips new or changed project hooks until the operator re-trusts them, so every engine update that touches the hook registration turns the engine's local guardrails OFF until /hooks re-approval. The engine says so at the moment it changes the registration; the status readout additionally warns when NO session in this project has run its hooks recently (a coarse net — a recent session on either runtime clears it for up to a day); and the per-session check is the floor's own grounding rule: verify the start-of-session briefing arrived, and treat the gates as off when it did not. Disclosure at three ranges, because no mechanism can re-trust on the operator's behalf.

The engine's two helper servers are registered on Codex with every tool auto-approved. Both servers are read-only today (memory exposes search; the wiring map exposes lookups), so nothing runs un-consented — but the grant is standing: a future tool added to either server would be auto-approved on Codex without a new consent moment, an asymmetry the Claude side (which approves per server, per app) does not share. Adding such a tool must revisit this entry and the approval mode together.

Codex's own documentation states its pre-tool hook is a guardrail rather than a complete enforcement boundary, and that interception of its newer shell path is incomplete — so the Explore write-gate on Codex can miss shell forms the platform does not surface. The protected main branch and the operator's merge remain the only wall, on every runtime.
